title: Chapter Thirty: The Injunction
content: The Court of Session is colder than I expected.

Not metaphorically — though it is that too, in the way institutional buildings always are, all brushed steel and indifferent stone. No, I mean physically. The air conditioning hums through the ceiling vents with a low, mechanical insistence, and by the time Sarah Chen spreads her papers across the counsel table, my fingers are stiff at my sides. I sit in the second row behind her, close enough to see the annotations in the margins of her briefs, close enough to smell the faint dry-erase marker scent still clinging to her fingertips from the whiteboard session we ran at four this morning.
I have never been on this side of a legal proceeding. The competent side.

At Bly, the law was something that happened to other people — people with money, people with names that appeared on deeds. I watched Henry Wingrave's solicitors arrive in their dark cars the way you watch weather: inevitable, impersonal, not something you could negotiate with. They came to process the estate after my parents died. They came to place Flora in Henry's guardianship. They came to take the house apart piece by piece and bill us for the privilege.
I was always on the other side of the table. The side that nod. The side that signs. The side that loses.
Today I am behind someone who does not lose. And the difference is so stark it makes my chest ache.

Sarah's team occupies the left side of the courtroom. Two junior counsel, both women, both wearing the kind of controlled stillness that I'm learning to recognize as a legal weapon — never fidget, never rush, let the silence do half the work. Sarah herself wears dark grey, minimal jewelry, her hair pulled back tight enough that it looks almost severe. She is not trying to be liked. She is trying to be precise.

On the opposite side: Henry Wingrave's QCs. Three of them, in full wig and gown, because the Court of Session still observes the theatre of it. They are men. They are expensive. They have the easy confidence of people who have been winning arguments in rooms like this since they were called to the bar, and they move through their papers with the practiced efficiency of surgeons prepping for a routine operation.
Henry himself is not present. Of course he isn't. Henry never enters a room he can have someone else enter for him.

The judge — Lady Drummond, as Sarah identified her in the corridor beforehand, a strict constructionist with a reputation for being unmoved by sentiment — enters, and we all rise. The sound of chairs scraping the stone floor echoes once and dies. The silence that follows has a particular weight. Not the silence of an empty room. The silence of a room full of people holding their breath.

"The petition," Lady Drummond says, and her voice is clearer than I expected, carrying to every corner without effort, "concerns the ownership and dissemination of audiovisual material recorded at Bly Manor, Essex, between the years 2018 and 2020. I have read the submissions. I will hear oral argument. Counsel for the petitioner."
Henry's lead QC rises. He is tall and silver-templed and he speaks in the measured cadence of a man who has been trained to make the outrageous sound reasonable.

"My Lady, the footage in question was recorded on equipment owned and maintained by the Bly Manor estate. It was installed for security purposes on private property. The recordings constitute estate property under the same legal principle that applies to any fixed asset — a security system is no different from a gate or a fence. The respondent has no claim to ownership, and the dissemination of this material — which includes images of a minor, my client's ward Flora Wingrave — constitutes violations of the Data Protection Act 2018, the UK GDPR, and the Children and Young Persons Act as it pertains to the protection of children from harmful publication."
He pauses. Lets the silence hold for exactly two beats.

"Moreover, my Lady, the footage has been distributed online in a manner that can only be described as reckless. It has been posted to internet forums, shared across social media platforms, and discussed in terms that — while I will not repeat them here — have caused significant distress to my client and pose a safeguarding risk to the child who appears in the material. The petitioner seeks return of the property, injunctive relief against further dissemination, and damages for the breach of privacy."
He sits. The rustle of his gown is the only sound.
Sarah rises.
She does not stand the way he stood. He stood like a man who belonged there. She stands like a woman who has decided to be there, and the distinction matters.

"My Lady, I do not dispute that the footage was recorded on estate equipment. I do not dispute that Flora Wingrave appears in portions of the material. What I do dispute — vigorously — is the petitioner's framing of this matter as a property dispute."
She places both hands flat on the table. Steadies herself the way a marksman steadies a rifle.

"The footage at issue contains evidence of a crime. The death of Rebecca Jessel, who died at Bly Manor in October 2020, was recorded — wholly or partially — by the security system the petitioner now claims as his property. The footage in question has been reviewed by the respondent and contains material directly relevant to the circumstances of Ms. Jessel's death. Destruction of this material would constitute spoliation of evidence in a potential criminal matter. Return of the material to the petitioner — the guardian of the estate and a person with a direct interest in the suppression of this evidence — would create an unacceptable risk of tampering or destruction."
She turns slightly, not toward the opposing counsel, but toward the judge. Eye contact. Direct.

"The public interest in the preservation of evidence of a suspected crime outweighs any property claim, however valid that claim might otherwise be. My Lady, the petitioner is correct that this is not a simple property dispute. But he is wrong about why. This is not a woman trying to steal a security tape. This is a woman trying to prevent a man from burying evidence."

The word *burying* lands in the courtroom like a stone dropped into still water. I watch Henry's QC — his face doesn't change, but his junior's pen stops moving for just a moment. A half-second hesitation. That's what Sarah was aiming for.

The argument continues. It goes back and forth the way arguments in courtrooms do — not dramatic, not cinematic, but grinding and technical and precise. Each side cites precedent. Each side objects. Lady Drummond listens with the patience of someone who has heard every variation of human dishonesty and is waiting for the one that surprises her.

Henry's QC returns to the child protection argument. He is smart to do so. The footage does contain images of Flora. The law is clear about the distribution of material featuring children. No amount of public interest framing can fully override a child's right to privacy. He knows this. He's using it the way Henry uses everything — as a wall to hide behind.

Sarah counters: proposed redaction of all footage featuring Flora. A protective order. Restricted access. Anything that preserves the evidentiary value without further exposing the child. She is offering compromises the way a chess player sacrifices pieces — strategically, each concession designed to make her essential position unassailable.

But I can see it, even if Sarah is too skilled to show it. Both sides are right. The footage is Henry's property. It is also evidence of a crime. The privacy concerns are real. The public interest concerns are also real. The law is not a knife that cuts cleanly. It is a net that catches everything and sorts it slowly, and in the sorting, things get lost.
The hearing ends. Lady Drummond reserves judgment. We wait.
The ruling comes down on a Thursday.

Sarah calls me from her office. Her voice is level, which is how I know it's bad news. When Sarah has good news, there's a slight increase in tempo, a brightening at the edges of her consonants. When she's managing expectations, she sounds exactly like this: calm, measured, clinical.
"The injunction has been granted," she says.
The words hit me like a physical impact. I grip the edge of the kitchen counter. The granite is cold under my palm. I notice that first — the cold — before I notice the anger.
"Partially," she adds, and there's that careful tone again. "Lady Drummond has ruled that the footage recorded on Bly Manor equipment constitutes estate property. Henry Wingrave retains ownership."
"He *owns* the evidence of what happened to Rebecca."

"He owns the recordings. But — and this is important, Maeve — the court has ordered preservation of all footage as evidence in an ongoing criminal investigation. The footage cannot be destroyed. It cannot be altered. It has been placed under a protective order that restricts access to authorized investigators and legal counsel."
"So he owns it but he can't touch it."
"Precisely."
I close my eyes. The kitchen is quiet. Through the window I can see the grey Edinburgh sky pressing down on the rooftops like a hand on a throat.
"He can't destroy it," I say slowly, working through the logic, "and we can't broadcast it."

"No. The injunction prohibits further dissemination. The online copies that have already been posted — the court can't unring that bell, but any further distribution would constitute contempt. The footage exists now in legal limbo. Owned by Henry. Protected by the court. Accessible to no one who might actually use it."

I think about r/BlyManorGame. I think about the Reddit threads, the TikTok analyses, the YouTube essays that picked apart every frame of that footage. I think about the internet's attention — the fickle, ferocious, magnificent thing that I spent weeks cultivating, the weapon I built with nothing but a laptop and the truth. And now that weapon has been taken from me. Not destroyed. Just made illegal to fire.
"Sarah," I say, and my voice sounds foreign to me, thin and exhausted, "this is a loss."

A pause. Then: "This is not over. The criminal investigation will continue. The preservation order ensures that. But we've lost the court of public opinion as an active front. We can't publish. We can't broadcast. We can't use the internet the way we've been using it. That avenue is closed."

I hear the honesty in it. She's not spinning. She's not managing. She's telling me exactly what happened, and the weight of it settles across my shoulders like a physical thing — like the cold in the courtroom, like the rustle of papers, like the silence after a ruling that gives nobody what they want.
"Come to the office," Sarah says. "There's something else."

Her office smells like coffee and old paper. The radiator beneath the window clicks and ticks, fighting the June chill that seems to have settled over Edinburgh like a grudge. Sarah closes the door behind me and moves to her desk, where a single envelope sits on the blotter, positioned with the precision of a surgeon's instrument.
"The injunction ruling came this morning," she says, not sitting, standing behind her desk with her arms folded. "But this came yesterday afternoon. I wanted to tell you in person."

She picks up the envelope and hands it to me. It's official. I can tell by the weight of the paper and the crest embossed in the top-left corner: a crown above a sword and sceptre, the motto *Justice* beneath. The Crown Office and Procurator Fiscal Service.
I open it. The language is dense, legal, impersonal. But I catch the essential phrases: *formal investigation*, *death of Rebecca Jessel*, *witness testimony*, *sufficient grounds*.
"The Crown Office is opening a formal investigation into Rebecca's death," I say, reading it again to be sure.
"Yes."
"Not because of the internet. Not because of the footage."
"No. Because a witness has come forward. Someone who was at Bly the night Jessel died. Someone who provided a statement to the Procurator Fiscal three days ago."

I look up from the letter. Sarah is watching me, and her expression is the one I've come to recognize as her holding something back — not out of cruelty, but out of care. She's deciding how much I can take at once.
"Who?" I say.
"Someone the internet never found," she says. "Someone who wasn't in the footage. Someone who's been silent for six years and has now — finally — broken that silence."
"Sarah."
"The witness is Margaret Grose."

The name lands in the room and doesn't echo. It sinks. It goes down through the floorboards and into the foundation and into the earth beneath, and I feel it settle somewhere in my body, deep below the ribs, in the place where I store the things I can't afford to drop.
Mrs. Grose.

Who held my hand at my parents' funeral. Who made Flora's lunches and checked her homework and sat with her through the long nights after we came to live at Bly. Who found Rebecca Jessel's room empty and told me so with those exact words — *empty, dear, nothing left but the bed* — and whose eyes, when she said it, I now realize were not the eyes of someone reporting a fact. They were the eyes of someone repeating a script.
She was there.
She was *there* the night Jessel died.
And she told me — told everyone — that she found the room empty. That there was nothing to find. That Rebecca was gone and there was nothing more to say about it.
She was lying. For six years, she was lying.

I set the letter down on the desk. My hands are steady, which surprises me. I would have expected them to shake. But the body is strange about these things. Sometimes the biggest revelations produce the smallest physical responses — a stillness that is the opposite of calm, a quiet that is the opposite of peace.
"She's been lying," I say.

"She's been silent," Sarah corrects. "There's a difference. She didn't come forward to defend Henry or to support his story. She came forward to tell the truth. Something has changed for her. Something made her decide that six years of silence was enough."
"Something like the footage being broadcast to the entire internet?"

"Perhaps. Or perhaps something else entirely. People's reasons for breaking silence are rarely straightforward. But the point is: she's talking now. She's given a statement. The Crown has found it credible enough to open a formal investigation. And that investigation exists independently of this injunction, independently of the internet, independently of everything we've built and everything we've lost today."

I think about Mrs. Grose. I think about her broad hands and her steady voice and the way she used to hum while she cooked — old songs, folk songs, nothing you could name. I think about the way she looked at Flora, with the particular tenderness of a woman who has chosen to love a child that isn't hers. I think about the way she looked at me, which was different — careful, measured, as though she was always calculating exactly how much of herself to give.

I think about the night Jessel died, and I think about Mrs. Grose's face in the morning when she came to my room and told me that the governess had gone, and I realize — with the slow, sick clarity of something that was always visible but never looked at — that the expression on her face that morning was not shock. It was exhaustion. The exhaustion of someone who has been awake all night carrying a weight they did not choose.

"The footage can't be broadcast," I say, and my voice is quiet now, the anger drained out of it, replaced by something else. Something that feels like the first cold breath of a door opening. "But Mrs. Grose can testify."
"Yes."
"She was there. She saw what happened."

"According to her statement, she saw —" Sarah pauses, and I see something shift in her face. Not hesitation. Anticipation. The look of someone who is about to hand you a key that fits a lock you've been kicking at for years. "She saw the whole thing, Maeve. And she's willing to say so under oath."

I stand in Sarah's office with the Crown Office letter in my hands and the injunction ruling still fresh in my chest, and I feel the shape of the day rearrange itself around me. The loss is real. The injunction is real. Henry owns the footage. The internet can't touch it. The most powerful weapon I've ever built has been legally neutralized, rendered inert by a system that is bigger and older and more patient than any of us.
But the system has its own weapons. And one of them has just been aimed at the same target I've been aiming at all along.

The alliance I built was always three women against the system: me, Sarah, and the ghost of Rebecca Jessel, whose death we refused to let the world forget. Now it's four. And the fourth is not a ghost. She is a woman who has been carrying the truth for six years like a stone in her pocket, and she has finally set it down.
Mrs. Grose was there the night Rebecca Jessel died. She saw everything.
What did she see?

The question hangs in the air between me and Sarah, and neither of us answers it, because answering it would mean guessing, and guessing would mean hoping, and hoping is a luxury we can't afford. We deal in evidence now. We deal in testimony. We deal in the slow, grinding machinery of the law, which is another Game with its own rules, its own strategies, its own terrible patience.

But I can feel it — beneath the loss, beneath the injunction, beneath the cold that has settled into my bones since I walked into that courtroom. A door is opening. Not the one I was pushing against. A different one. One I didn't even know was there.
And on the other side of it, Margaret Grose is waiting.
